You are doing a read-only code review of the repo at "C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-tracking". Do NOT edit any files.

Read all .md documentation files and read through the entire source code (all .js/.ts files), skipping node_modules, .git, dist, coverage, and lock files. Read every source file — this is meant to be exhaustive, not a sample.

Find and report:
1. Bugs — wrong logic, unhandled errors, race conditions, security issues (injection, missing auth checks, leaked secrets), null/undefined crashes, incorrect async handling.
2. Inefficient code — N+1 queries, queries inside loops, loading whole collections to filter in memory, repeated work, missing batching, blocking synchronous calls.
3. Poorly written code — duplicated blocks, dead code, confusing names, functions doing too many things, copy-paste drift, inconsistent error handling, magic numbers.
4. Documentation problems — .md files that contradict the actual code or are clearly stale.

For each finding give: file path and line number, severity (high = real bug or security issue / medium = likely problem or significant inefficiency / low = style or minor cleanup), a one-sentence plain-English explanation of what's wrong and what it could cause, and confidence (certain / likely / possible). Only report things you actually verified by reading the code — no speculation.

Your final message is the deliverable: a structured list of findings grouped by severity, plus a 2-3 sentence overall assessment of the codebase's health.

# Designflow Tracking

Tracking backend service for the Designflow PLM platform (production tracking, licensing
tracking, lead-time calculation, and **sample tracking**). Node/Express + Sequelize +
PostgreSQL. The service is private and sits behind the BFF; the browser reaches it at
`/api/tracking/*`.

## Branch workflow

Work only on `sandbox-albert`. Before adding code, sync GitHub `develop` into GitHub
`sandbox-albert`, pull GitHub `sandbox-albert` locally, then make changes. Push commits to
`sandbox-albert` and keep the PR from `sandbox-albert` to `develop` updated or create it if it
is missing. Never merge to `main`.

Add or update unit tests for new behavior before committing.

## Documentation map

| Doc | Read it when |
|---|---|
| [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) | **Always first** — canonical guide: structure, custom-code boundary, data model, quirks, deployment, pending work |
| [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) | Claude Code-specific notes |
| [`docs/api-reference.md`](docs/api-reference.md) | You need the current tracking API route inventory |
| [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) | System design, request flow, sample-tracking model |
| [`docs/development.md`](docs/development.md) | Local setup, run, test, debug |
| [`docs/configuration.md`](docs/configuration.md) | Environment variables and config files |
| [`docs/deployment.md`](docs/deployment.md) | Cloud Build → Cloud Run pipeline |

## Stack

- Node.js (CommonJS), Express
- PostgreSQL + Sequelize (models are the schema source of truth; no separate migration tool)
- JWT (RS256) auth via the BFF; DigitalOcean Spaces for sample photos

## Quick start

```bash
npm install
npm test          # Jest unit tests
npm run start     # NODE_ENV=localhost (runs npm test first, then nodemon)
```

Requires `.env.localhost` with DB and JWT variables — see [`docs/configuration.md`](docs/configuration.md).

## Health check

`GET /api/health`

## System interconnectivity

This repo is the tracking service in the larger Designflow PLM system. It is
reached through BFF `/api/tracking/*` and owns production, licensing, lead-time,
and sample tracking while sharing item/order/user/factory data with the other
services through PostgreSQL. See
[`docs/system-interconnectivity.md`](docs/system-interconnectivity.md) for the
tracking-specific map and [`docs/system-architecture.md`](docs/system-architecture.md)
for the full multi-repo system map.
# CLAUDE.md — designflow-tracking

Read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) first — it is the canonical guide. This file holds only
Claude Code-specific notes.

## Memory and branch

- User memory: `C:\Users\ahazan2\.claude\projects\D--repos-dflow\memory\MEMORY.md`
- Active branch: `sandbox-albert`. Work only on `sandbox-albert` (and `develop` for syncing).
  Before new code, confirm local `sandbox-albert`, fetch GitHub, merge `origin/develop` into
  local `sandbox-albert`, push that updated `sandbox-albert` to GitHub, then pull GitHub
  `sandbox-albert` locally before editing. Never commit to or merge into `main`. Commit locally,
  push to `origin/sandbox-albert`, then ship via PR `sandbox-albert` -> `develop`, updating or
  creating it after pushes.

## Ignore guidance

Use `.claudeignore` (mirrors `.cursorignore`). It excludes `node_modules/`, `dist/`, caches,
logs, and Playwright artifacts from AI context.

## Allowed operations

- Allowed: read/edit repo files, `npm install`, `npm test`, `node`, `git add/commit`,
  `git push origin sandbox-albert`.
- Not allowed: editing `node_modules/`; `git push --force`; committing to `main`.

## Tests

- `npm test` (Jest, `tests/**/*.test.js`). Run before every commit — `start:*` scripts run
  `npm test` first, so a red suite blocks boot.
- Add/extend a unit test for any function you add or change (mock Sequelize via
  `jest.mock('../../models/db')` and `helpers/auth`; see `tests/unit/sample.model.test.js`).

## Deployment / SSH

Deployment is Cloud Build → Artifact Registry → Cloud Run (see `AGENTS.md` §11). SSH is **not**
part of the workflow.

## Commit style

```
type(scope): short description
```
Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `chore`. Scope e.g. `sample`, `tracking`, `migration`.

## Claude-specific behaviors

- Keep the `models/db.js` synchronous-populate-then-await ordering (see AGENTS.md §9).
- snake_case columns only in raw `ALTER TABLE` DDL.
- Derive sample status changes from `helpers/sampleStatus.js`; keep the frontend mirror
  (`pages/sample_tracking/helpers/sample.status.ts`) in sync.
# AGENTS.md — designflow-tracking

Canonical operating guide for developers and AI sessions working in this repo.
Read this first; it should give you a working mental model in under 5 minutes.

## 1. Project summary

`designflow-tracking` is a Node/Express + Sequelize + PostgreSQL backend service in the
Designflow PLM platform. It serves the **tracking** domain used by the Angular frontend
(`designflow-frontend`) via the BFF: production tracking, licensing tracking, lead-time
calculation, and — added in this work — **sample tracking** (physical product samples moving
between factories and the Ningbo/NYC offices). The service is private (Cloud Run
`--no-allow-unauthenticated`); all browser traffic reaches it through the BFF at
`/api/tracking/*`.

## Multi-model AI note

There is no universal ignore-file standard across AI coding tools.

`.claudeignore` works for Claude Code.

When using any other AI tool, paste this file as your first message and follow the instructions in the "What to ignore" section.

## Documentation map: what to read for each task

Always start with:

- `AGENTS.md`

Then load additional docs only when relevant:

| Task / question | Read these docs | Usually do not need |
|---|---|---|
| Quick repo orientation | `README.md`, `AGENTS.md` | `docs/` deep dives |
| Modify endpoints / model logic (incl. sample tracking) | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md` | `docs/deployment.md` unless deploy changes |
| Change env vars / config / secrets | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/configuration.md`, `docs/deployment.md` if prod runtime affected | unrelated architecture docs |
| Local setup, run, test, debug | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/development.md` | `docs/deployment.md` |
| Deploy / Docker / Cloud Build / rollback | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/deployment.md`, `docs/configuration.md` | `docs/development.md` |
| DB tables / migrations / data flow | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/configuration.md` if env affected | `docs/deployment.md` unless rollout changes |
| Investigate a bug/incident | `AGENTS.md` (§12 Critical incidents), the area's doc, `HANDOFF.md` if present | unrelated docs |
| Continue unfinished work | `AGENTS.md`, `HANDOFF.md` (only if present) | docs outside the handoff scope |
| Claude Code session | `CLAUDE.md`, then `AGENTS.md` | other docs unless needed |
| Docs-only cleanup | `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, affected `docs/` | source except to verify |

This routes work to the few relevant docs — do not ingest every `.md`. Update this table whenever a doc is added, removed, or repurposed. There is currently **no `HANDOFF.md`** in this repo (no unfinished work); if one appears, it is required reading for continuation tasks.

## 2. Repository structure

| Path | What it is | Ownership |
|---|---|---|
| `index.js` | Express bootstrap, CORS, `x-user-authorization`→`authorization` swap, startup caches, `app.listen` | code we own |
| `routes/` | Express routers mounted in `routes/index.js` (`/`, `/lic`, `/lead-time`, `/sample`, `/api`) | code we own |
| `controllers/` | Thin HTTP handlers that call models and shape responses | code we own |
| `models/*.model.js` | Domain logic (callback-style: `Method = async (req, result) => result(err, data)`) | code we own |
| `models/db.js` | Sequelize bootstrap + idempotent migration block | code we own |
| `models/db/*.js` | Sequelize table definitions (one file per table), auto-loaded by `init-models.js` | code we own |
| `helpers/` | auth (JWT), digitalocean (S3/Spaces), utility, lead-time, sampleStatus state machine | code we own |
| `config/` | `db.config.js`, `digitalocean.config.js`, `env.js`, `columnOrderConfig.js` | code we own |
| `tests/unit/` | Jest unit tests (mocked Sequelize / helpers) | code we own |
| `tests/e2e/` (Playwright) | Playwright config exists; e2e is not the active path | code we own |
| `Dockerfile`, `cloudbuild.yaml` | Build + Cloud Run deploy | deployment files |
| `docs/` | This documentation set | docs |
| `node_modules/` | Installed deps (gitignored) | third-party |

There is no separate ORM migration tool: **the Sequelize models are the schema source of
truth** (`sequelize.sync()` creates tables), with an awaited idempotent `ALTER TABLE ... ADD
COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` block in `models/db.js` for additive column changes.

## 3. Prime Directive: custom-code boundary

Our custom code lives here:

- `models/`, `controllers/`, `routes/`, `helpers/`, `config/`
- `tests/`
- `docs/`
- `Dockerfile`, `cloudbuild.yaml`

Everything under `node_modules/` is third-party and must not be edited. New domain features
follow the existing **router → controller → model** layering; do not put DB logic in
controllers or HTTP logic in models.

## 3a. Branch, sync, test, and PR workflow

All feature work for this sandbox happens on `sandbox-albert`.

Before adding new code:
1. Confirm the local branch is `sandbox-albert`.
2. Fetch GitHub.
3. Bring the latest `origin/develop` into GitHub's `sandbox-albert` by merging `origin/develop` into local `sandbox-albert`, then pushing `sandbox-albert` to GitHub.
4. Pull GitHub's `sandbox-albert` back into local `sandbox-albert`.
5. Only then add or change code.

Branch rules:
- Work only on `sandbox-albert`.
- Use `develop` only as the source branch for syncing and the PR base.
- Never commit to, merge into, or open PRs against `main`.
- Do not force-push.

Quality gate:
- Add or update unit tests for every function or behavior you create or change.
- Run the relevant Jest tests before committing; `npm test` is the preferred final check.

PR rule:
- Commit locally on `sandbox-albert`, then push to `origin/sandbox-albert`.
- Keep the GitHub PR for `sandbox-albert` -> `develop` updated after pushing.
- If the PR does not exist, create it with `develop` as the base and `sandbox-albert` as the head.

## 4. Core modification inventory

No files outside the project-owned areas above were modified. (The whole repo is
project-owned; there is no vendored/generated code checked in.)

## 5. Task-to-file navigation

| Task | Files to touch | Files NOT to touch |
|---|---|---|
| Add a tracking endpoint | `routes/<x>.router.js`, `controllers/<x>.controller.js`, `models/<x>.model.js` | `node_modules/` |
| Add a DB table | new `models/db/<table>.js` + association in `models/db/init-models.js` (sync creates it) | already-applied data |
| Add a column to a table | the `models/db/<table>.js` field + an `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` line in `models/db.js` `applySampleMigrations` (snake_case only) | rename existing columns |
| Change sample status rules | `helpers/sampleStatus.js` (mirror in frontend `pages/sample_tracking/helpers/sample.status.ts`) | hard-code statuses in controllers |
| Add a sample photo/comment/box feature | `models/sample*.model.js`, `controllers/sample*.controller.js`, `routes/sample.router.js` | shared `comments`/`itemAttachment` tables (samples use their own) |
| Mount a new router | `routes/index.js` | — |

## 6. Data model and external identifiers

Sample tracking owns these tables (schema = `process.env.SCHEMA`, e.g. `designflow`):

| Entity | Identifier / key | Where defined | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sample | `sample_id_pk` | `models/db/sample.js` | origin, direction, status, office_location, qty, retail_price, fob_cost; links `item_id_fk`→itemHeader, `prod_order_no_fk`→ProdOrderHeader |
| Sample event | `event_id_pk` | `models/db/sample_event.js` | append-only check-in/out + status history |
| Sample comment | `id` | `models/db/sample_comments.js` | `sample_id_fk`, TEXT body, threaded via `parent_id` |
| Sample photo | `sample_attachment_id` | `models/db/sample_attachment.js` | `attachment_link` = DigitalOcean Spaces public URL |
| Factory group | `factory_group_id_pk` | `models/db/sample_factory_group.js` | groups samples from one factory |
| Box | `box_id_pk` | `models/db/sample_box.js` | Ningbo↔NYC shipment; status independent of member samples |
| Shipment item | `shipment_item_id_pk` | `models/db/sample_shipment_item.js` | sample↔box membership per leg |

`GET /sample/list` also returns two **derived** (non-stored) fields per row for the grid:
`primary_photo` (the sample's primary `sample_attachment.attachment_link`) and `box_label`
(the `sample_box.box_label` for the sample's current `box_id_fk`). See `models/sample.model.js`.

External identifiers:

| System | Identifier | Where | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grid skin | `grid_id = 'sample_tracking'` | `GridAccessLevel`/`GridLayout` rows | NOT seeded yet; frontend uses a hardcoded column config (see Pending work) |

## 9. Intentional quirks and non-obvious decisions

### Samples are their own tables, not `itemHeader` rows

Looks like: licensing tracking hangs off the shared `itemHeader`, so samples "should" too.

Actually: samples are stored in dedicated `sample*` tables (production-tracking style).

Why: `itemHeader` is owned by the item-master sync (`designflow-data-syncing` `findOrCreate`).
Injecting samples there would pollute the item master and risk them appearing as real items.

Do not change because: it would couple samples to an externally-synced table and break the
item master. Cross-module links are done via `sample.item_id_fk` / `prod_order_no_fk` instead.

### `db.js` populates the export synchronously, then awaits

Looks like: `initialize()` is `async` but called without `await`, and `db` is mutated, not returned.

Actually: model definitions are assigned to the exported `db` object **before** the first
`await` (Object.assign), then authenticate/sync/migrations are awaited.

Why: `index.js` `startServer()` reads `db.LicenseFeedBacks.findAll()` synchronously right after
`require('./models/db')`. Awaiting before populating `db` leaves it empty at boot → crash.

Do not change because: reordering an `await` above `Object.assign(db, …)` reintroduces the boot
crash. Guarded by `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js`.

### Status is derived server-side, with an optimistic concurrency guard

Looks like: the client could send the target status.

Actually: `sample.model.recordEvent` derives `to_status` from
`(direction, current status, office, event_type)` via `helpers/sampleStatus.js`, then updates
with `WHERE sample_id_pk = ? AND status = <from_status>`; 0 affected rows → HTTP 409.

Why: two users checking the same sample in/out must not both succeed; the client must not be
able to force an illegal transition.

Do not change because: trusting a client-supplied status or dropping the `WHERE status=` guard
reintroduces race conditions and illegal jumps.

### snake_case columns only in raw DDL

Looks like: other tables use mixed-case column names.

Actually: all sample tables + the `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` block use snake_case.

Why: unquoted mixed-case identifiers fold to lowercase in Postgres DDL and orphan the column.

Do not change because: a mixed-case `ADD COLUMN` would silently create the wrong column.

142	# Architecture — designflow-tracking
143	
144	## Overview
145	
146	A single Express service exposing the Designflow tracking domain. It is private (Cloud Run
147	`--no-allow-unauthenticated`) and is reached only through the BFF.
148	
149	```
150	Browser (Angular: designflow-frontend)
151	  → designflow-bff  (terminates user JWT, attaches Cloud Run OIDC token,
152	                     forwards user JWT as x-user-authorization)
153	  → designflow-tracking  (/api/tracking/* → service root paths)
154	  → PostgreSQL (Sequelize)        + DigitalOcean Spaces (sample photos)
155	```
156	
157	`index.js` first copies `x-user-authorization` back onto `authorization` so route guards
158	(`helpers/auth.js authRole`) see the user JWT even though the BFF replaced `authorization` with
159	the Cloud Run OIDC token.
160	
161	## Request layering
162	
163	```
164	routes/<x>.router.js     authRole([...]) gate, path → controller
165	controllers/<x>.controller.js   thin: unwrap req, call model, map errors to HTTP status
166	models/<x>.model.js      domain logic, callback style: (req, result) => result(err, data)
167	models/db.js → models/db/*.js   Sequelize models (schema source of truth)
168	```
169	
170	`routes/index.js` mounts: `/` (lib), `/lic` (licensing), `/lead-time`, `/sample`, `/api` (health).
171	
172	## Persistence and migrations
173	
174	- `models/db.js` builds the Sequelize instance, populates the exported `db` object
175	  **synchronously** (model definitions don't need a live connection), then awaits
176	  `authenticate()` → `sync()` → `applySampleMigrations()`.
177	- `sequelize.sync()` creates brand-new tables from the models. Additive column changes go in
178	  the idempotent `applySampleMigrations()` block (`ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS`,
179	  snake_case only). There is no separate migration CLI.
180	
181	## Sample tracking
182	
183	The sample module is modelled on production tracking (its own tables) rather than licensing
184	(shared `itemHeader`), because `itemHeader` is owned by the item-master sync.
185	
186	**Tables** (`models/db/`): `sample`, `sample_event`, `sample_comments`, `sample_attachment`,
187	`sample_factory_group`, `sample_box`, `sample_shipment_item`. Associations are wired in
188	`models/db/init-models.js`.
189	
190	**Lifecycle / state machine** (`helpers/sampleStatus.js`): a sample has an `origin`
191	(factory_request / factory_offer / usa_bought) that sets its `direction`:
192	
193	- inbound: `requested → shipped → ningbo_checked_in → ningbo_checked_out → nyc_checked_in`
194	- outbound: `nyc_requested → nyc_checked_out → ningbo_checked_in → ningbo_checked_out → sent_to_factory`
195	- shared extras (any time): `cancelled`, `on_hold`, `damaged`, `approved`
196	
197	`recordEvent` derives the next status from `(direction, current status, office, event_type)`,
198	writes it under an optimistic guard (`WHERE status=<from_status>`, 0 rows → 409), and appends a
199	`sample_event` row. Boxes carry their own status independent of member samples; adding a
200	direction-mismatched sample to a box is skipped and reported (warn, not block).
201	
202	**Grouping**: `sample.factory_group_id_fk` (factory shipment group) and `sample.box_id_fk`
203	(current box cache) plus `sample_shipment_item` for per-leg box membership (a box can bundle
204	samples from multiple factory groups).
205	
206	**Integration links**: `sample.item_id_fk` (→ `itemHeader`) and `sample.prod_order_no_fk`
207	(→ `ProdOrderHeader`) let licensing/production attach samples to their records.
208	
209	**Photos**: presigned-URL upload to DigitalOcean Spaces (`helpers/digitalocean.js`):
210	`POST /sample/getPreSignedUrl` → browser PUT → `POST /sample/setObjectPublic` →
211	`POST /sample/photos` stores the public URL in `sample_attachment`.
212	
213	## Constraints
214	
215	- Service must stay private; the BFF is the only ingress.
216	- FKs are application-enforced (not DB constraints); the model layer owns cascade/integrity
217	  (e.g. `Sample.remove` deletes child events/comments/photos/shipment-items).
218	- `npm test` runs before boot in every `start:*` script.
219	
220	---
221	
222	## Cross-repo system map
223	
224	Tracking is reached through `designflow-bff` at `/api/tracking/*`. It owns
225	production, licensing, lead-time, and sample tracking while sharing item,
226	production order, user, vendor, factory, merch group, and grid metadata with the
227	other services through PostgreSQL. See
228	[`system-interconnectivity.md`](system-interconnectivity.md) for the
229	tracking-specific interconnectivity map and
230	[`system-architecture.md`](system-architecture.md) for the full
231	multi-repo architecture.

282	## Pipeline
283	
284	Cloud Build (`cloudbuild.yaml`) runs three steps:
285	
286	1. **Build** — `docker build -t ${_IMAGE} --build-arg NODE_ENV=${_APP_ENV} .`
287	   (`Dockerfile`: `node:lts-alpine`, installs `imagemagick`, `yarn` install, `CMD yarn
288	   start:$NODE_ENV` — which runs `npm test` then starts the server).
289	2. **Push** — pushes `${_IMAGE}` to Artifact Registry. Image path:
290	   `${_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/${_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/${_APP_ENV}/${_APP_NAME}:${BUILD_ID}-${COMMIT_SHA}`.
291	3. **Deploy** — `gcloud run deploy ${_SERVICE_NAME}` with `--no-allow-unauthenticated`
292	   (private), `--vpc-connector`, `--service-account`, `--memory`, `--region`, `--port`,
293	   `--concurrency`, `--max-instances`.

295	## Secrets and env at deploy
296	
297	- `--set-secrets`: `DB_USER`, `DB_PASSWORD`, `DB_NAME`, `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`,
298	  `JWT_PRIVATE_KEY`, `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY`, `DO_ACCESS_KEY`, `DO_SECRET_KEY` (all from Secret Manager
299	  `projects/$PROJECT_NUMBER/...`; the `deployer@` runtime SA has project-level secretAccessor).
300	- `--set-env-vars`: `WEB_URL`, `API_ADDRESS`, `SCHEMA`, `DO_ENDPOINT_URL`, `DO_BUCKET_NAME`,
301	  `DO_PUBLIC_URL` (the `DO_*` ones come from `_DO_*` trigger substitutions).
302	- `_DO_ENDPOINT_URL`/`_DO_BUCKET_NAME`/`_DO_PUBLIC_URL` are **defaulted in `cloudbuild.yaml`**
303	  to the shared `dflowbucket` Spaces values (identical in sandbox + prod, verified against the
304	  core services), so no trigger change is needed. A trigger-level `_DO_*` substitution still
305	  overrides the defaults if a different bucket is ever required.
306	
307	## Trigger and substitutions
308	
309	All `_*` substitutions (`_REGION`, `_SERVICE_NAME`, `_APP_ENV`, `_APP_NAME`,
310	`_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME`, `_PORT`, `_CONCURRENCY`, `_MAX_INSTANCES`, `_VPC_CONNECTOR`,
311	`_PLATFORM`, `_MEMORY`, `_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME`, `_WEB_URL`, `_API_ADDRESS`, `_DB_SCHEMA`) are
312	provided by the **Cloud Build trigger** (one per environment), not hard-coded in the repo. The
313	build runs when the branch the trigger watches receives a push.
314	
315	## Rollback
316	
317	Redeploy a previous image tag to the Cloud Run service:
318	
319	```
320	gcloud run deploy <service> --image <region>-docker.pkg.dev/<project>/<repo>/<env>/<app>:<previous-build-id>-<sha> --region <region>
321	```
322	
323	## Access / SSH
324	
325	The service is private; the **BFF** is the only ingress (it attaches the Cloud Run OIDC token).
326	There is no SSH-based deploy or admin path — deployment is image-based via Cloud Build/Cloud Run.
327	
328	## Pre-deploy gate
329	
330	`yarn start:$NODE_ENV` runs `npm test` first; a failing test suite stops the container from
331	starting. Keep the suite green before pushing the deploy branch.

384	npx jest tests/unit/sample.model.test.js   # one file
385	npx jest --watch              # watch mode
386	```

388	Tests mock Sequelize and helpers — they do not need a database:
389	
390	```js
391	jest.mock('../../models/db', () => ({ sample: { findByPk: jest.fn(), update: jest.fn(), /* ... */ } }));
392	jest.mock('../../helpers/auth', () => ({ getUserInfo: jest.fn(async () => ({ user_id: 1, name: 'X' })) }));
393	```
394	
395	Always add or extend a unit test when you add/change a function. Source-string guard tests live
396	in `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` (table/column presence, init ordering, snake_case DDL).
397	
398	## Debugging tips
399	
400	- 403/401 from a route → the user JWT role isn't in the route's `authRole([...])` list, or the
401	  `x-user-authorization` header wasn't forwarded by the BFF.
402	- 409 from `POST /sample/events` → optimistic concurrency: the sample's status changed since you
403	  read it (or an illegal transition). Re-read and retry.
404	- Startup crash reading `db.LicenseFeedBacks` → ensure `models/db.js` still populates `db`
405	  (Object.assign) **before** the first `await` (see AGENTS.md §9).

525	# Designflow PLM System Architecture
526	
527	This document maps the Designflow product lifecycle management system across the
528	local repositories under `D:\repos\dflow`. It is written from the code as it
529	exists in this workspace, not from the GitHub repo names alone.
530	
531	The important correction to the informal "five repo" model is that the runtime
532	PLM system has six active application/service repos locally:
533	
534	- `designflow-frontend`
535	- `designflow-bff`
536	- `designflow-backend`
537	- `designflow-item-master`
538	- `designflow-tracking`
539	- `designflow-data-syncing`
540	
541	The original five named repos are the main subject, but `designflow-item-master`
542	is an active service in the request path. The frontend calls it through the BFF,
543	and the BFF has a first-class `item_master` service mapping. Any complete system
544	map should include it.
545	
546	## Executive Summary
547	
548	Designflow is a Cloud Run based PLM application. The browser runs a static
549	Angular SPA. The SPA does not call private backend services directly in deployed
550	environments; it calls the BFF at `api.*.designflow.app`. The BFF proxies each
551	request to one of the private domain services, adding a Cloud Run OIDC token for
552	service-to-service invocation while preserving the user's Designflow JWT in
553	`X-User-Authorization`.
554	
555	Most services use:
556	
557	- Node.js CommonJS
558	- Express
559	- PostgreSQL
560	- Sequelize
561	- `SCHEMA` to select the logical database schema
562	- RS256 Designflow JWTs for user identity and role checks
563	
564	The main persistent coupling between services is the shared PostgreSQL schema.
565	The HTTP/API coupling is intentionally centralized through the BFF.
566

649	## Repository Responsibilities
650	
651	### `designflow-frontend`
652	
653	The frontend is the Angular 21 SPA. It owns:
654	
655	- UI routing and feature modules
656	- Role-based client navigation and route guards
657	- AG Grid based work surfaces
658	- RFQ calculation behavior in the browser
659	- User JWT storage in `localStorage`
660	- HTTP interceptors for auth, loading, and error reporting
661	- Microsoft SSO browser flow before exchanging the Azure ID token for a
662	  Designflow JWT
663	
664	The deployed frontend calls the BFF, not individual backend services directly.
665	The environment files define these service bases:
666	
667	- `environment.url` -> `/api/core`
668	- `environment.item_master` -> `/api/item_master`
669	- `environment.tracking` -> `/api/tracking`
670	- `environment.data_sync` -> `/api/data_sync`
671	
672	Major user-facing modules:
673	
674	- RFQ
675	- Item Library
676	- Item Detail
677	- Art Piece management
678	- Standardized Products
679	- Production Tracking
680	- Licensing Tracking
681	- Sample Tracking
682	- Lead Time Calculator
683	- Admin / Editor
684	- User Management and impersonation
685	- Notifications
686	- HTS / tariff / AI classification UI
687	
688	### `designflow-bff`
689	
690	The BFF is the public API gateway for the SPA. It owns:
691	
692	- `/health`
693	- `/api/:service/*`
694	- service key mapping
695	- Cloud Run OIDC identity token generation
696	- original user JWT forwarding
697	- deployed CORS configuration
698	
699	Service keys:
700	
701	- `core` -> `designflow-backend`
702	- `item_master` -> `designflow-item-master`
703	- `tracking` -> `designflow-tracking`
704	- `data_sync` -> `designflow-data-syncing`
705	
706	The BFF strips the service prefix before proxying. For example:
707	
708	```text
709	Browser:
710	  GET /api/tracking/sample/list
711	
712	BFF target:
713	  GET /sample/list on designflow-tracking
714	```
715	
716	Cloud Run OIDC audience must be the backend service `.run.app` URL, not the
717	custom `api.*.designflow.app` domain. This is why the BFF environment variables
718	must use backend `.run.app` URLs.
719	
720	### `designflow-backend`
721	
722	The core backend owns broad PLM business logic and shared administration. It is
723	the densest service and includes both legacy root routes and newer `/api/*`
724	routes.
725	
726	It owns:
727	
728	- Azure SSO token exchange and Designflow JWT issuing
729	- Password/signup/single-use token flows
730	- RFQ grid and vendor quote workflows
731	- RFQ view/grid layout persistence
732	- Factory/vendor management support
733	- Customers, item sizes, item depths, delivery locations, FOB countries
734	- Standardized product APIs
735	- Comments and audit logs
736	- Notifications and mention notifications
737	- Admin app settings
738	- Tariff management: MFN HTS lookup, Section 301, Trump 2025 rates
739	- OpenRouter proxy for AI classification
740	- CBP CROSS proxy
741	- Brevo transactional email
742	- DigitalOcean Spaces presign/public URL helpers
743	- Impersonation tokens
744	
745	Representative route groups:
746	
747	- `/auth`
748	- root legacy RFQ routes such as `/getAllItem`, `/updateRfqItem`,
749	  `/updateVendor`, `/sendPriceReq`
750	- `/std`
751	- `/api/admin`
752	- `/api/openrouter`
753	- `/api/cross`
754	- `/api/comment`
755	- `/api/notifications`
756	- `/api/user`
757	- `/api/customers`
758	- `/api/grid`
759	- root DigitalOcean routes such as `/getPreSignedUrl`, `/setObjectPublic`
760	
761	### `designflow-item-master`
762	
763	This service is not in the original five-link prompt, but it is wired into the
764	frontend and BFF as an active service. It owns item master workflows that are no
765	longer solely in the core backend.
766	
767	It owns:
768	
769	- Item Library APIs under `/lib`
770	- Item Detail APIs under `/detail`
771	- Art Piece APIs under `/artPiece`
772	- Item creation and item type lookup
773	- Product assignment APIs
774	- Item and art piece media/attachment workflows
775	- Item detail licensing status helpers
776	
777	Representative frontend consumers:
778	
779	- `ItemLibraryService`
780	- `ItemDetailService`
781	- `ArtPieceService`
782	
783	### `designflow-tracking`
784	
785	Tracking is a separate private service for lifecycle tracking after or around
786	product/item creation.
787	
788	It owns:
789	
790	- Production tracking root routes
791	- Licensing tracking routes under `/lic`
792	- Lead-time lookup and calculation under `/lead-time`
793	- Sample tracking under `/sample`
794	- Sample events, boxes, groups, comments, photos
795	- DigitalOcean Spaces upload flow for sample photos
796	- Startup caches for licensing and factory lead-time calculations
797	
798	Representative route groups:
799	
800	- `/getProdOrderHeader`
801	- `/updateProdOrderHeader`
802	- `/factoryProdOrders`
803	- `/factoryProdOrderDates`
804	- `/syncPrepackAssociations`
805	- `/lic/getLic`
806	- `/lic/groups`
807	- `/lead-time/calculate`
808	- `/sample/list`
809	- `/sample/events`
810	- `/sample/boxes`
811	- `/sample/photos`
812	
813	### `designflow-data-syncing`
814	
815	Data Sync is a private system-to-system integration service for Coldlion sync
816	flows. It is not a normal user-facing route surface.
817	
818	It owns:
819	
820	- Pulling item headers from Coldlion
821	- Pulling item details from Coldlion
822	- Pulling merch group headers/details
823	- Pulling production order headers/details
824	- Pulling external vendors/customers
825	- Syncing prepack associations
826	
827	It uses `X-API-Key: <DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY>` for every request, including health.
828	It also has Coldlion API credentials injected through secrets.
829	
830	## Request and Auth Flow
831	
832	```mermaid
833	sequenceDiagram
834	  participant U as User / Browser
835	  participant FE as Angular SPA
836	  participant BFF as designflow-bff
837	  participant SVC as Private backend service
838	  participant DB as PostgreSQL
839	
840	  U->>FE: Uses PLM feature
841	  FE->>BFF: HTTP request with Authorization: Bearer DesignflowJWT
842	  BFF->>BFF: Resolve service key from /api/:service/*
843	  BFF->>BFF: Fetch Google OIDC token for backend .run.app URL
844	  BFF->>SVC: Authorization: Bearer CloudRunOIDC
845	  BFF->>SVC: X-User-Authorization: Bearer DesignflowJWT
846	  SVC->>SVC: Copy X-User-Authorization back to Authorization
847	  SVC->>SVC: authRole validates RS256 Designflow JWT and role
848	  SVC->>DB: Sequelize query/update
849	  DB-->>SVC: Rows
850	  SVC-->>BFF: JSON response
851	  BFF-->>FE: JSON response
852	```
853	
854	This dual-token pattern is the key to understanding deployed authentication:
855	
856	- Cloud Run sees the OIDC token and allows the BFF to invoke the private service.
857	- Express route guards see the restored user JWT and enforce product roles.
858	
859	## Deployment Model
860	
861	Cloud Build builds each repo into a container image and deploys it to Cloud Run.
862	
863	Public services:
864	
865	- `designflow-frontend`: public static SPA
866	- `designflow-bff`: public API entrypoint
867	
868	Private services:
869	
870	- `designflow-backend`
871	- `designflow-item-master`
872	- `designflow-tracking`
873	- `designflow-data-syncing`
874	
875	Common private service deployment traits:
876	
877	- VPC connector configured
878	- `--no-allow-unauthenticated`
879	- DB secrets injected from Secret Manager
880	- `SCHEMA` injected through environment substitutions
881	
882	The core backend additionally injects:
883	
884	- JWT private/public keys
885	- DigitalOcean Spaces keys
886	- Brevo API key
887	- OpenRouter / DeepSeek keys
888	- Azure client/tenant IDs
889	
890	The data-sync service additionally injects:
891	
892	- `DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY`
893	- `COLDLION_API_KEY`
894	
895	## Shared Data Model and Coupling
896	
897	The services are split by API ownership, but they are strongly connected through
898	the same database schema. Shared model families include:
899	
900	- Users, roles, permissions
901	- Vendors/factories
902	- Customers
903	- Merch groups
904	- UDF tables/elements/components
905	- Grid layouts and grid access
906	- Item header/detail/attachments
907	- RFQ items, groups, vendors, steps, statuses, containers
908	- Standardized products
909	- Production order header/detail
910	- Licensing status/milestone/feed-back/time records
911	- Sample tracking tables
912	- External customers/vendors
913	
914	This means a feature often crosses service boundaries through common table
915	identifiers even when there is no direct service-to-service HTTP call. For
916	example, Sample Tracking stores `item_id_fk` and `prod_order_no_fk` links that
917	connect samples back to item master and production records.
918	
919	## Feature Area Map
920	
921	```mermaid
922	flowchart TB
923	  subgraph UX["Frontend feature modules"]
924	    RFQ["RFQ"]
925	    IL["Item Library"]
926	    ID["Item Detail"]
927	    AP["Art Piece"]
928	    STD["Standardized Products"]
929	    PT["Production Tracking"]
930	    LT["Licensing Tracking"]
931	    ST["Sample Tracking"]
932	    LTC["Lead Time"]
933	    Admin["Admin / Editor"]
934	  end
935	
936	  RFQ --> Core["Core backend"]
937	  STD --> Core
938	  Admin --> Core
939	  IL --> ItemMaster["Item Master"]
940	  ID --> ItemMaster
941	  AP --> ItemMaster
942	  PT --> Tracking["Tracking"]
943	  LT --> Tracking
944	  ST --> Tracking
945	  LTC --> Tracking
946	
947	  Core --> DB["PostgreSQL"]
948	  ItemMaster --> DB
949	  Tracking --> DB
950	  Sync["Data Sync"] --> DB
951	  Sync <--> CL["Coldlion"]
952	```
953	
954	## End-to-End Flows
955	
956	### Sign-In and Identity
957	
958	Password login and Microsoft SSO both end by storing a Designflow RS256 JWT in
959	the browser. For SSO, the browser obtains an Azure ID token, posts it to
960	`/api/core/auth/azure`, and the core backend verifies Azure JWKS before signing a
961	Designflow JWT.
962	
963	Every subsequent browser request sends the Designflow JWT to the BFF.
964	
965	### RFQ and Vendor Quoting
966	
967	RFQ is primarily a frontend + core-backend workflow:
968	
969	1. Frontend loads grid layout and RFQ rows from core backend.
970	2. Frontend applies AG Grid column definitions and calculation value setters.
971	3. User edits RFQ item fields or vendor quote fields.
972	4. Frontend recalculates landed cost, margins, duty, container cost, and sales
973	   pricing where relevant.
974	5. Core backend persists RFQ item/vendor changes.
975	6. Brevo may send quote/signup/request emails.
976	7. Vendor users access filtered RFQ data through the same BFF/core path.
977	
978	RFQ also reaches into other domains:
979	
980	- Factory country data is used by tariff calculations.
981	- Item images/attachments may be stored in DigitalOcean Spaces.
982	- Grid layouts and view states are shared system metadata.
983	- Standardized products can export/import to RFQ.
984	
985	### Item Master and Art Piece
986	
987	Item Library, Item Detail, and Art Piece screens are frontend modules that route
988	through `/api/item_master`.
989	
990	The item-master service owns the active API boundary for these screens. It reads
991	and writes item master tables, while data-sync can populate or refresh item data
992	from Coldlion into the same schema family.
993	
994	### Production and Licensing Tracking
995	
996	Production and licensing tracking route through `/api/tracking`.
997	
998	Production tracking works with production order tables and factory-facing order
999	date updates. Licensing tracking works with licensing status/milestone/grouping
1000	data and item license images. The tracking service builds startup caches for
1001	licensing/factory lead-time calculations.
1002	
1003	### Sample Tracking
1004	
1005	Sample Tracking is a newer tracking module under `/api/tracking/sample`. It has
1006	its own tables and state machine rather than using `itemHeader` directly as the
1007	primary record. It links to the broader PLM through:
1008	
1009	- `item_id_fk`
1010	- `prod_order_no_fk`
1011	- factory/group references
1012	- sample events
1013	- sample boxes
1014	- sample attachments/photos
1015	
1016	The server is authoritative for sample status transitions.
1017	
1018	### Lead-Time Calculation
1019	
1020	The frontend calls `/api/tracking/lead-time/*`. The tracking service uses design
1021	team time, licensing time, factory time, merch group descriptions, and fuzzy
1022	matching helpers to calculate lead time and needed dates.
1023	
1024	### HTS, Tariffs, and AI Classification
1025	
1026	The frontend duty-rate tools call core backend admin/openrouter/cross routes.
1027	
1028	Core backend:
1029	
1030	- proxies OpenRouter chat/model calls
1031	- proxies CBP CROSS search
1032	- looks up bundled MFN HTS rates
1033	- manages Section 301 upload/lookup state
1034	- manages Trump 2025 country-rate settings
1035	- stores AI/tariff app settings
1036	
1037	The model suggests classification. The system derives actual rates from
1038	authoritative lookup sources and admin-maintained settings.
1039	
1040	### Coldlion Sync
1041	
1042	Data Sync is the Coldlion integration layer. It is API-key protected and intended
1043	for service/system usage.
1044	
1045	It pulls external data into the same database schema used by the application
1046	services. That makes sync a background/source-of-truth feeder for item,
1047	merch-group, vendor/customer, production-order, and prepack-association data.
1048	
1049	## Operational Notes
1050	
1051	- The app has no single OpenAPI contract across all services.
1052	- Many routes are internal Angular-shaped APIs and return domain rows or
1053	  grid-specific payloads.
1054	- BFF routes are stable as a gateway, but service contracts remain mixed
1055	  legacy/new style.
1056	- The shared database is the strongest cross-service coupling.
1057	- Additive schema changes are commonly handled by startup Sequelize sync and
1058	  idempotent inline migrations rather than a dedicated migration repository.
1059	- JWT is stored in browser `localStorage`, not an HTTP-only cookie.
1060	- DigitalOcean direct browser upload uses a presigned PUT followed by an
1061	  explicit `setObjectPublic` call.
1062	
1063	## Canonical Local Ports
1064	
1065	| Service | Repo | Local port |
1066	|---|---|---:|
1067	| Frontend | `designflow-frontend` | 4200 |
1068	| BFF | `designflow-bff` | 5004 |
1069	| Core backend | `designflow-backend` | 5000 |
1070	| Data sync | `designflow-data-syncing` | 5001 |
1071	| Tracking | `designflow-tracking` | 5002 |
1072	| Item master | `designflow-item-master` | 5003 |
1073	
1074	## Canonical BFF Service Prefixes
1075	
1076	| Browser path prefix | Target service |
1077	|---|---|
1078	| `/api/core/*` | `designflow-backend` |
1079	| `/api/item_master/*` | `designflow-item-master` |
1080	| `/api/tracking/*` | `designflow-tracking` |
1081	| `/api/data_sync/*` | `designflow-data-syncing` |
1082	
1083	## Where To Change Things
1084	
1085	Use this as a first-pass guide:
1086	
1087	- Frontend-only layout, grid behavior, labels, navigation: `designflow-frontend`
1088	- RFQ data, vendor quoting, users, roles, admin settings, tariffs, SSO:
1089	  `designflow-backend`
1090	- Item Library / Item Detail / Art Piece APIs: `designflow-item-master`
1091	- Production / licensing / sample / lead-time APIs: `designflow-tracking`
1092	- Coldlion import/sync behavior: `designflow-data-syncing`
1093	- API routing, deployed service target, Cloud Run OIDC forwarding, CORS:
1094	  `designflow-bff`
1095	
1096	# System Interconnectivity
1097	
1098	The tracking service owns production, licensing, lead-time, and sample tracking.
1099	It is private in deployed environments and is reached through the BFF at
1100	`/api/tracking/*`.
1101	
1102	## Runtime Position
1103	
1104	```mermaid
1105	flowchart LR
1106	  FE["designflow-frontend"]
1107	  BFF["designflow-bff"]
1108	  Tracking["designflow-tracking"]
1109	  DB["PostgreSQL"]
1110	  DO["DigitalOcean Spaces"]
1111	  Core["designflow-backend<br/>shared RFQ/admin data"]
1112	  ItemMaster["designflow-item-master<br/>item data"]
1113	  Sync["designflow-data-syncing<br/>Coldlion order/item data"]
1114	
1115	  FE --> BFF
1116	  BFF -->|/api/tracking/*| Tracking
1117	  Tracking --> DB
1118	  Tracking --> DO
1119	  Core -.shared schema.-> DB
1120	  ItemMaster -.shared schema.-> DB
1121	  Sync -.syncs into schema.-> DB
1122	```
1123	
1124	## Feature Ownership
1125	
1126	| Area | Route group | Notes |
1127	|---|---|---|
1128	| Production tracking | `/` root routes | Production order header/detail views and updates. |
1129	| Factory production updates | `/factoryProdOrders`, `/factoryProdOrderDates` | Vendor/factory-facing production date updates. |
1130	| Licensing tracking | `/lic/*` | Licensing rows, statuses, images, and groups. |
1131	| Lead-time | `/lead-time/*` | Design team, licensing, and factory time lookups plus calculation. |
1132	| Sample tracking | `/sample/*` | Samples, events, comments, boxes, groups, photos, and status state machine. |
1133	
1134	## Inter-Service Couplings
1135	
1136	- Production and licensing rows depend on item, vendor, merch group, and
1137	  production order data that may also be used by core backend and item-master.
1138	- Data-sync imports production order and external reference data from Coldlion
1139	  that tracking screens consume.
1140	- Sample tracking intentionally has its own tables, but links back to the wider
1141	  PLM with `item_id_fk` and `prod_order_no_fk`.
1142	- Sample photos use DigitalOcean Spaces through a presigned URL flow similar to
1143	  core backend media workflows.
1144	- The frontend reaches this service only through BFF `/api/tracking`, and the BFF
1145	  forwards the Designflow JWT through `X-User-Authorization`.
1146

content.text:
I've now read all documentation and every source file in the repo. Here is the full review.

# Code review: designflow-tracking

## HIGH severity

1. **`models/lib.model.js:541-546` — Hardcoded third-party API key committed in source** (certain)
   The ColdLion API key `Z21355JALT13A54L9X5` and endpoint `http://x5.coldlion.com/...` are hardcoded in `syncPrepackAssociations`, over plain HTTP. Anyone with repo access has a live production credential, and it can't be rotated without a code change. Everything else in this stack correctly uses Secret Manager / env vars.

2. **`routes/lead-time.router.js:6-16` — All lead-time routes have no auth middleware** (certain)
   Every other router wraps routes in `authRole([...])`; the lead-time router has none, so `PUT /lead-time/design-team-times/:id`, `/licensing-times/:id`, and `/factory-times/:id` let any caller who reaches the service (no JWT, any role) modify master lead-time templates that drive production needed-date calculations. The PUTs also pass `req.body` straight to `update()` (mass assignment).

3. **`models/lib.model.js:278, 353` and `models/lic.model.js:208, 304, 389` — Errors returned as HTTP 200 success** (certain)
   The catch blocks call `result(null, err)` instead of `result(err)`, so DB failures in `getProdOrderHeader`, `updateProdOrderHeader`, `getAllLicenses`, `updateLic`, and `getGroupsByIds` are sent to the grid as a 200 response whose body is the error object — the frontend silently renders garbage or appears to "succeed" on a failed update.

4. **`models/lib.model.js:307-336` and `models/lic.model.js:258-287` — `getViewlayout` has no error handling and can hang the request** (certain)
   Both copies have no `.catch`; a DB error produces an unhandled rejection and the HTTP request never gets a response (hangs until client timeout). Additionally `data[i].dataValues.GridLayouts[0]` throws if a `GridAccessLevel` row has no joined layout. The `getUserInfo` result on the first line is fetched and never used.

5. **`index.js` + `controllers/lead-time.controller.js:177-237` — Startup caches are never refreshed after template updates** (certain)
   `LicensingTimeCache`, `MgLicTimesMap`, and `MgFactoryTimesMap` are built once at boot (`index.js:45-94`), but the lead-time PUT endpoints update those same tables and never invalidate the caches. All production-tracking needed-date/delay calculations keep using stale numbers until the next deploy/restart.

## MEDIUM severity

6. **`models/lib.model.js:493-501` — `updateFactoryProdOrderDates` nulls out every date not present in the request** (likely)
   The payload coerces each missing/falsy field to `null` unconditionally, so a partial update (e.g. only `actual_etd`) silently erases the other six factory dates on the order.

7. **`models/lib.model.js:172-179` — Production grid `filterModel` is parsed and then ignored** (certain)
   `getProdOrderHeader` parses `filterModel` into `filters` but never applies it to `whereClause`; server-side grid filtering on production tracking does nothing (rows always match only the `%COS` exclusion).

8. **`models/lib.model.js:258-274` — Deduplication after pagination breaks counts/pages** (likely)
   Rows are deduplicated by `prodOrderNo` *after* `limit/offset` were applied, while `totalCount` comes from a separate unduplicated `count`. Pages can come back short and the grid total disagrees with rendered rows.

9. **`models/sample.model.js:150-161, 346-357`, `models/sampleComment.model.js`, `models/sampleMedia.model.js:71-82` — No vendor scoping on by-ID sample reads (IDOR)** (likely)
   `Sample.list` carefully restricts `vendor` users to their own `factory_id_fk`, but `GET /sample/:id`, `/:id/events`, `/:id/comments`, and `/:id/photos` (all READ-role, vendor included) do no such check — a factory portal user can enumerate IDs and read other factories' samples, prices (`fob_cost`, `retail_price`), comments, and photos.

10. **`models/sampleComment.model.js:41` — Client-supplied `user_id` overrides the authenticated user** (certain)
    `user_id: body.user_id ?? user?.user_id` lets any caller attribute a comment to another user. Combined with comment update/delete having no ownership check (`sampleComment.model.js:52-79` — any READ-role user can edit/delete anyone's comment), the comment audit trail isn't trustworthy.

11. **`models/sample.model.js:257-275, 283-344` and `models/sampleGroup.model.js:170-183` — Multi-step deletes/state changes are not transactional** (certain)
    `Sample.remove` deletes events/photos/comments/shipment-items before checking the sample exists and without a transaction (a mid-way failure orphans data; deleting a nonexistent ID still purges any stray children). `recordEvent` updates the status and then inserts the audit event separately — a crash in between leaves a status change with no event row. Same pattern in `Box.remove`.

12. **`models/sampleGroup.model.js:209-230` — Queries inside a loop when adding samples to a box** (certain)
    One `sample_shipment_item.create` + one `sample.update` per sample ID instead of `bulkCreate` + one bulk `update`; 50 samples = 100 sequential queries.

13. **`models/lib.model.js:520-586` — `syncPrepackAssociations` is an N+1 cascade** (certain)
    Sequential ColdLion HTTP call per prepack code, then `itemHeader.findOne` per sub-item per code, then `upsert` per (sub-item × pkey). No batching anywhere; with many prepacks this holds a request open for minutes.

14. **`controllers/lead-time.controller.js:43-79, 93-95` — Duplicated fuzzy-matcher with dangerous fallback drift** (certain)
    This file re-implements `helpers/leadTimeUtils.fuzzyMatch` but adds a step 4 that **falls back to `records[0]`** when nothing matches — `calculateLeadTime` will silently compute lead time from an arbitrary unrelated licensor/product (labelled only `matchType: 'default'`). It also reloads all three template tables from the DB on every calculate request instead of using the startup caches.

15. **`models/db/init-models.js:274-302` — Fire-and-forget DB query during synchronous model init** (likely)
    `UDFTable.findAll()` runs before `authenticate()` has resolved, has no `.catch` (a failure is an unhandled rejection), and the UDF-driven associations are wired asynchronously — requests arriving early can see missing associations; a bad `UDFTable_associate_type` value would call an arbitrary/undefined model method.

16. **`helpers/auth.js:4-6` — JWT verify option typo: `algorithm` instead of `algorithms`** (certain)
    The option key is wrong, so the RS256 restriction is silently ignored (jsonwebtoken v9's key-type check is the only thing preventing HS256 algorithm-confusion; on an older jwt lib this would be a critical hole). `models/lib.model.js:5` spells it correctly — copy-paste drift. Also `getUserInfo` (auth.js:44-52) awaits `jwt.verify` with a callback, which only works by accident of jsonwebtoken's internals and returns `false` instead of an error on bad tokens.

## LOW severity

17. **`helpers/imageMagick.service.js:15` — Calls `utility.EmptyFolder`, which doesn't exist** (certain) — would throw `TypeError` if ever invoked; the whole file is dead code (never imported), as are `helpers/multer.js` and `models/obj/ProdHeader.module.js` (which also leaks an implicit global via `return item = {...}`).
18. **`e2e/example.spec.js` — stock Playwright demo testing playwright.dev** (certain) — dead scaffold, tests nothing in this service.
19. **`config/env.js:7` — `(process.env.NODE_ENV).toLowerCase()` crashes with a confusing TypeError if NODE_ENV is unset** (certain); the comment above it lists envs (`local, develop, production`) that don't match the actual scripts (`localhost, sandbox, sandbox-albert, production`).
20. **`index.js:16, 20, 25-35` — CORS configured three times** (certain): `cors()` mounted twice plus a manual header middleware; `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on a service whose only legal caller is the BFF.
21. **`models/db.js:13-24` — extreme magic numbers** (certain): `acquire: 600000` (10 min), `connectTimeout: 3000000` (50 min) hide connectivity failures for a very long time; also `throw 'Unable to connect...', error` (line 42) uses the comma operator, throwing only the bare error and discarding the message string.
22. **`models/lib.model.js:284, 340, 506` and `models/lic.model.js:159, 234, 291` — `logging: console.log` left enabled on hot-path queries** (certain) — dumps full SQL for every grid load/update in production logs.
23. **`models/db/users.js:82` and `models/db/merchGroup.js:78` — `schema: process.env.schema` (lowercase)** (likely) — undefined on Linux/Cloud Run; works only because the global search_path covers it; inconsistent with `SCHEMA` everywhere else.
24. **`controllers/lic.controller.js:101-106` — `createGroup` missing `return` after the 400 response** (certain) — falls through to call the model and can attempt a second response.
25. **`models/lic.model.js:333-372` — not-found in `updateGroup`/`deleteGroup` is mapped to HTTP 500 instead of 404** (certain); `getGroupsByIds` controller hardcodes 500 for all errors.
26. **`models/lic.model.js:215-231` — `mapFieldToColumn` falls back to the raw client-supplied field name as a where-clause key** (possible) — unknown AG Grid colIds become Sequelize column references; worst case is a 500, but it also permits `$association.column$` traversal syntax.
27. **Unused imports/dead requires throughout** (certain): `fs`, `mime-types`, `path`, `sleep`, `response` (from express!), `authToken` in `lib.model.js`/`lic.model.js`; `bcrypt`, `jwt`, `fs`, `path`, and `columnOrderConfig` in `helpers/utility.js` (`reorderColumns` at utility.js:1631-1640 takes `gridId` and the config but ignores both — `config/columnOrderConfig.js` is entirely dead); `jest` is listed under `dependencies` instead of `devDependencies` in package.json.
28. **`models/sample.model.js:62` — number `inRange` filter uses `filterDef.filterTo` without a null check** (possible) — `Op.between [n, undefined]` produces a DB error if AG Grid sends a half-filled range; the date branch guards this, the number branch doesn't.
29. **Duplicated blocks**: `getGridHeader` exists verbatim in both `lib.model.js:282-305` and `lic.model.js:233-256`; AG Grid `buildCondition` is duplicated between `sample.model.js` and `lic.model.js` (certain).

## Documentation problems

30. **`CLAUDE.md:7` and `docs/system-architecture.md:528`** — both reference `D:\repos\dflow` as the workspace/memory path; the actual workspace is `C:\repos\dflow plm`. Stale (certain).
31. **`AGENTS.md` §2** — lists `tests/e2e/ (Playwright)`, but the Playwright suite lives at `e2e/` in the repo root (certain).
32. **`docs/configuration.md:277` / `AGENTS.md` §2** — describe `config/columnOrderConfig.js` as live "Grid column ordering config"; it's required but never read by any code path (certain).
33. **`docs/api-reference.md:79-91`** — the lead-time route table is the only one with no Auth column, and the doc's blanket statement "Most routes require a Designflow JWT" papers over the fact that these mutating routes require none (ties to finding #2) (certain).

## Overall assessment

The repo is two codebases in one: the newer sample-tracking module (models, state machine, tests) is well-structured, defensively written, and genuinely tested, while the older production/licensing code (`lib.model.js`, `lic.model.js`, `helpers/utility.js`) carries serious operational defects — errors returned as 200s, unhandled-rejection request hangs, ignored grid filters, a committed API key, and heavy copy-paste duplication. The most urgent items are rotating and externalizing the ColdLion key, adding `authRole` to the lead-time router, and fixing the `result(null, err)` / missing-catch error handling in the two legacy models; after that, cache invalidation for lead-time templates and vendor scoping on sample by-ID reads are the highest-value fixes.
